headline: SOYBEAN OIL BREAKS HARD, EXPORTS ON DECK
subheadline: A 6.7% drop in soybean oil reshapes the oilseed complex ahead of Thursday's 7:30 AM export sales print.
lead: Soybean oil fell hard Thursday, dropping 6.7% to $66.08, the biggest single-session move in the complex in weeks, and it dragged the whole oilseed story with it. Soybeans eased to $11.41 on both nearby and November, meal gave back half a percent to $303.10, and the setup now sits squarely on this morning's USDA Weekly Export Sales at 7:30 AM CT. Wednesday's announced sale of 13.7 million bushels to unknown destinations gave beans a reason to run yesterday; today's export sales number either validates that buyer or leaves the chart holding the bag. The thread this week has been about whether the Belt caught up to seasonal pace: crop condition reports from the field say yes, but export demand is the other half of the price equation, and that answer comes in hours.
the_takeaway: Soybean oil's 6.7% drop is the session story; exports at 7:30 decide if beans follow.
teaser: Soy oil breaks 6.7%, exports print at 7:30 AM. Positioning now.

one_number.unit: bushels of soybeans sold Wednesday to unknown destinations
one_number.context: USDA announced the sale Wednesday afternoon, the bulk earmarked for 2026-27 delivery. That sale pushed beans higher into yesterday's close, but it also raises the question: is this one buyer building a position quietly, or the start of a sustained demand wave tied to China's $17 billion annual purchase commitment announced May 18? Today's export sales report at 7:30 AM CT will either add a chapter to that story or leave the mystery exactly where it is.

yesterdays_call.summary: Yesterday's high-conviction call: cattle bounced on two simultaneous supply constraints finally pricing together, not on hope.
yesterdays_call.note: Live cattle flat at $249.05 today, no follow-through either way; the supply-constraint thesis hasn't broken down but hasn't extended either.

sections.title: SOYBEAN OIL BREAKS
sections.body: Soybean oil fell 6.7% to **$66.08**, the day's dominant story and the kind of single-session move that reorganizes the entire oilseed complex. The Iran-Hormuz tensions, with the Strait of Hormuz premium that built since early April now deflating on diplomatic progress, matter here: more than 60 million barrels of crude sitting in the Persian Gulf are preparing to move to Asian markets as the chokepoint reopens, and that crude flow feeds directly into the vegetable oil price competition. When crude stays bid and palm and other competing oils ease, soybean oil loses its energy-linkage premium fast. Soybeans dropped to **$11.41** on both nearby and November, a 6.5-cent drag. Meal at **$303.10** held better, only a half-percent softer, which tells you the sell pressure was concentrated in the oil share of the crush, not in demand for protein. The 13.7-million-bushel sale to unknown destinations announced Wednesday is the counterweight: whoever bought that beans is not selling the story short. Export sales at 7:30 AM this morning are the next chapter.
sections.catalyst: 60 million barrels of Hormuz crude heading to Asia pressures soybean oil's energy-linkage premium.
sections.bottom_line: Oil share of the crush just repriced lower; meal held. Export sales resolve the bean direction.
sections.farmer_action: Old-crop beans in storage: watch the 7:30 print before making any cash sales. Under 300K MT in today's export sales and the nearby stays under pressure.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: yesterday's 13.7M-bushel sale tailwind gave way to soy oil breakdown.

sections.title: CORN DRIFTS, WAITS
sections.body: Corn eased less than 2 cents to **$4.18** nearby, **$4.46** December, and both contracts are doing exactly what the field reports suggest they should: not much. A north-central Iowa farmer interviewed Wednesday described planting as done by early May, herbicide applications wrapping up, and crops looking healthy. That is not the description of a market that needs a weather premium. The 2026 planting season, behind average through May before catching up between rain systems, has now largely normalized, and with pollination still weeks away, there is no urgency on the upside. The export sales print this morning covers corn too; the Belt-on-pace story keeps a lid on any weather bid, but a strong export number would remind the market that demand is still the other variable in the equation.
sections.catalyst: Field reports confirm healthy crop development; no weather threat to price a premium against.
sections.bottom_line: Corn is coiled inside a narrow range; export demand is the only near-term catalyst.

sections.title: CATTLE HOLDS, HOGS RUN
sections.body: Live cattle barely moved at **$249.05**, feeders drifted a tenth of a percent to **$367.43**, and after two days of sharp moves the complex is catching its breath. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout that began May 19 is still in the background, the 1,700 locked-out workers still processing roughly 6,000 fewer head per day, and USDA's announcement Wednesday of a new APHIS administrator comes as the New World screwworm situation in Texas continues without a comprehensive response plan. Beef Magazine noted this week that political responses alone won't solve screwworm, and leadership gaps at APHIS have left the cattle industry without a clear path. Neither of those issues pushed the needle today. Hogs were the livestock story Thursday, rising 1.6% to **$96.55** on no single clean catalyst: looks like fund rotation into a contract that had been lagging the broader livestock recovery.
sections.catalyst: APHIS administrator named amid screwworm scrutiny; Cargill lockout continues with no resolution announced.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle taking a breath; screwworm and lockout both still in the price structure.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: yesterday's bounce held but didn't extend; thesis intact, momentum flat.

sections.title: MACRO TURNS DEFENSIVE
sections.body: Gold dropped 1.8% to **$4,263**, silver fell 2.9% to **$67.28**, and the S&P gave back 1.2% to **$7,420.10**, all on the same day the US Dollar Index firmed 0.6% to **$100.80**. That combination, metals and equities lower, dollar higher, is a classic risk-off rotation, and it is not friendly to grain exports priced in dollars. A stronger dollar makes US bushels more expensive for foreign buyers, which is exactly the wrong backdrop for a Thursday export sales report the market is counting on. India's energy import bill reportedly gained sharply year-over-year in May, and that kind of demand pressure for energy in Asia is one reason the macro picture is getting complicated. Watch the dollar: if it holds above **$100.50** through next week, the export window the China deal opened gets narrower without a corresponding price concession.
sections.catalyst: Dollar firmed 0.6% as metals and equities pulled back; risk-off ahead of export sales report.
sections.bottom_line: Dollar strength is the quiet headwind behind this morning's export sales number.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Moving FAS Workforce Out of DC to Kansas City
outside_the_pit.body: USDA announced Wednesday it will relocate much of the Foreign Agricultural Service's DC-based staff to Kansas City and Beltsville as part of a broader reorganization. The FAS is the agency that negotiates trade access and tracks export commitments including the China $17 billion deal; disrupting institutional memory and relationships mid-cycle is a real operational risk that won't show up in Friday's futures close.

outside_the_pit.title: Dairies Get H-2A Clarity, But the Seasonal Catch Stays
outside_the_pit.body: DHS and DOL confirmed Wednesday that dairy operations can use the H-2A temporary worker program, but positions must still qualify as temporary or seasonal, a constraint that covers little of the year-round labor dairy actually needs. Dairies across the Upper Midwest and West that were hoping for a broader labor fix are still looking for one.

outside_the_pit.title: 60 Million Barrels Leaving the Gulf as Hormuz Reopens
outside_the_pit.body: More than 60 million barrels of crude oil backed up in the Persian Gulf are preparing to move to Asian markets as Strait of Hormuz traffic resumes. That flow will reprice energy in Asia and take more pressure off the crude-linked vegetable oil complex, reinforcing the soybean oil move today and keeping fertilizer input costs lower than they were six weeks ago.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybean oil / soybean meal share of crush value
spread_to_watch.level: Oil share compressed sharply Thursday on the 6.7% drop; meal held relative value.
spread_to_watch.commentary: When oil and meal diverge this sharply in a single session, the crush margin math changes and processors re-evaluate run rates. If oil doesn't recover toward $70 in the next few sessions, crushers have less incentive to run hard, which eventually tightens meal supply and could support meal prices even as bean futures stay soft. Watch whether the oil/meal ratio normalizes or holds the gap.

basis.headline: Corn basis softening in Western Belt; Eastern bid firmer on ethanol.
basis.body: **Eastern Belt corn basis is firming** modestly as ethanol plants maintain steady grind after earlier maintenance windows. Western Belt basis is staying soft, consistent with the seasonal pattern and healthy crop development in the Northern Plains. Soybean basis is quiet ahead of the export sales print; the 13.7-million-bushel Wednesday sale hasn't moved the physical market yet, but a strong export number at 7:30 could tighten interior bids by midday.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the Belt caught up to seasonal pace after May's wet start?
weekly_thread.status_text: Field reports this week, including a north-central Iowa grower describing planting done by early May and healthy crop development, confirm the Belt caught up and then some. The question now shifts: pace is confirmed, but export demand has to validate the price. Today's 7:30 AM export sales print is the next real data point.

the_more_you_know.title: The oil share of the crush: what $66 soybean oil actually means for the farmer.
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 6.7% drop in soybean oil to $66.08 matters beyond the futures board because the value of a bushel of soybeans is split at crush between meal and oil, and the ratio between them shifts constantly. When oil's share of crush value compresses, processors earn less per bushel processed, and over time that pressure works backward into the cash bid for beans at the elevator. The current move was driven by the unwinding of the energy linkage premium, competing vegetable oils getting cheaper as Hormuz crude flows resume, rather than a demand breakdown. That distinction matters: a structural demand drop is bearish for months; a premium unwind can stabilize once the new crude price is fully priced in. Producers sitting on old-crop beans should watch whether oil stabilizes above $65 or keeps sliding; the floor for that stabilization is the non-energy equilibrium for global vegetable oil, and nobody has a clean number for that right now.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybeans above 400K MT validates Wednesday's 13.7M-bushel sale and keeps November beans near $11.41; under 300K MT and the soy oil break becomes the dominant narrative for the week.
USDA Weekly Export Sales: corn above 800K MT adds a demand-side bid that the Belt-on-pace crop story alone isn't providing; under 500K MT and corn stays range-bound through the weekend.
Soybean oil: watch whether $65 holds as support after today's break. Below $65 and crush margins tighten further, pressuring the bean board.
Dollar Index: holding above $100.50 through next week narrows the export window for all row crops regardless of what today's sales print shows.
USDA Crop Progress: corn rated below 70% good-to-excellent would reintroduce a weather premium as pollination windows approach; above 75% and the demand side stays the only price driver.

daily_quote.text: To change and to change for the better are two different things.

source_summary: CME Group settlement prices, USDA NASS Crop Progress, USDA Export Sales announcements, Brownfield Ag News, OilPrice.com, EIA Short Term Energy Outlook, The Fence Post, Farm Policy News, Feedstuffs, Beef Magazine.
